meta.name: Mateo Complementary Feeding Guidance
meta.source: IIPA 'Safe Baby Food' Consumer Education Monograph (2015) + WHO / IAP / Government of India infant & young child feeding (IYCF) principles. Brand-neutral and homemade-first.
meta.notes: Ages are months after birth. All foods are HOMEMADE and brand-neutral per the IMS Act 1992 — no formula, milk substitutes, or commercial/packaged baby-food brands anywhere in this file. General educational guidance only, NOT medical advice or a meal plan.

principles: Exclusive breastfeeding for the first 6 months — no water, no other milk, no solids.
Start complementary (solid) foods at around 6 months, while continuing to breastfeed.
Homemade, freshly cooked family food gives the best variety of tastes and textures.
Feeding guidance is brand-neutral: this app never suggests formula, milk substitutes, or any baby-food brand.
Every baby is different — follow your baby's cues and your pediatrician's advice.

under_six_months.headline: Exclusive breastfeeding
under_six_months.guidance: For the first 6 months, breast milk is the only food and drink your baby needs — not even water. Breast milk protects against infections and is perfectly suited to your baby's gut, which is still maturing. Solids before about 6 months can be poorly digested and may raise the risk of illness and allergies.
under_six_months.if_not_breastfeeding: If you are not breastfeeding, or have any questions about feeding your baby, your pediatrician is the right person to guide you. This app does not recommend formula or any brand.
readiness: Can sit up with little or no support
Has lost the tongue-thrust reflex (no longer pushes food straight back out)
Shows active interest in the food others are eating
under_six_months.readiness_note: Teeth are NOT required to start solids. Look for these signs around 6 months rather than going by age alone.

stages.label: 6–8 months
stages.texture: Smooth purées and mashed food, thick enough to stay on a spoon
stages.frequency: 2–3 small meals a day, building up gradually, alongside breastfeeding on demand
stages.amount: Start with 2–3 spoonfuls and increase slowly as your baby shows interest
tips: Introduce one new food at a time and wait about 3 days before the next, watching for any reaction.
Keep breastfeeding on demand — food is in addition to milk, not a replacement yet.
No added salt or sugar is needed.

stages.label: 9–11 months
stages.texture: Mashed food with soft lumps, plus soft finger foods to pick up
stages.frequency: 3 meals a day plus 1–2 small snacks, alongside breastfeeding
stages.amount: About half a cup at each meal, guided by your baby's appetite

Fruits: Soft fruit pieces — banana, mango, papaya

tips: Offer soft finger foods so your baby learns to self-feed.
Continue one-new-food-at-a-time for foods that commonly cause reactions (egg, fish, peanut, dairy).
Still no added salt or sugar.

stages.label: 12–24 months
stages.texture: Chopped family food; your toddler can mostly self-feed
stages.frequency: 3 meals plus 2 healthy snacks a day
stages.amount: About three-quarters to one cup at each meal

Fruits: A variety of seasonal fruits, chopped small
Dairy: Boiled / pasteurised cow's milk as a drink
Egg & non-veg (if your family eats it): Chicken — well cooked and chopped small

tips: Your toddler can now share most family meals — keep them mildly spiced and not too salty.
Cow's milk (boiled or pasteurised) can be a drink now, but should not crowd out a balanced diet of solids.
Eat together — toddlers learn eating habits by copying the family.

never_feed.why: Risk of infant botulism — avoid completely before 12 months.

never_feed.item: Raw or unpasteurised milk and milk products
never_feed.why: May carry bacteria that cause serious illness in babies.

never_feed.item: Whole nuts, whole grapes, hard raw vegetables, popcorn, hard sweets
never_feed.why: Choking hazards — grate, mash, or chop small instead.

never_feed.item: Home-canned food, or food from dented, rusted, bulging, leaking or unlabelled tins/jars

never_feed.why: A baby's kidneys can't handle much salt, and sugar builds unhealthy taste preferences early.

never_feed.item: Tea, coffee, sugary drinks and packaged fruit juices
never_feed.why: No nutritional value and can reduce appetite for milk and nourishing food.

safety: Wash your hands and all utensils before preparing your baby's food.
Wash fruits and vegetables thoroughly under clean running water.
Cook meat, poultry and fish thoroughly to kill bacteria.
Use freshly cooked food where possible; refrigerate leftovers and use within 24 hours (meat/egg) or 48 hours (fruit/veg).
Reheat until piping hot all the way through, then cool before feeding — and reheat only once.
Test the temperature on the inside of your wrist before feeding.
Throw away any food left in your baby's bowl — never re-store food a spoon or your baby's saliva has touched.
Never leave prepared food at room temperature for more than about 2 hours.
Always supervise feeding, with your baby seated upright — never lying down or unattended.
feeding_note: Homemade, freshly cooked family food gives your baby the best variety of tastes and textures and is far healthier than packaged baby food. This app is brand-neutral and never suggests formula, milk substitutes, or any baby-food brand. For anything about your baby's nutrition, your pediatrician is the right guide.
